title: Endpoint Pattern — NxGN Actions
purpose: **AI Summary**: Management API endpoints follow the Actions pattern: Save (upsert), Get, GetGridList, Delete, Lookup, and Lifecycle/Action. Admin delete endpoints intentionally support collection-level bulk DELETE with Id+RowVersion items and entity-level single DELETE with route id+RowVersion body. All management endpoints extend TenantEndpoint base classes that extract TenantId, UserId, RequestLanguageId, and DefaultLanguageId from JWT claims. Commands return CommandResult variants; endpoints check result.Succeeded and call SendProblemAsync on failure.

GetGridListEndpoint — Paginated List Example 5
DeleteEndpoint - Collection Bulk Delete
description: Use for admin list pages that support multi-select deletion. The request carries one row version per selected row.

examples.name: DeleteEndpoint - Bulk Delete Request
description: Bulk admin deletes use an item collection instead of a bare Guid array so optimistic concurrency is checked per row.

description: Admin capture lookup endpoints return the aggregate lookup payload required by an edit page.

await auditService.LogAsync(

    payload: new { actionType.Code },
    ct: ct,
    dbContext: dbContext);

examples.name: Endpoints do not duplicate command-owned audit
description: Endpoints only translate command results into HTTP responses.

examples.name: Delete endpoints delegate audit to commands
description: Delete endpoints do not loop selected keys for audit. The command logs state changes with the active shared ActionsDbContext.

examples.name: Request Lifecycle Example 1
examples.code:
HTTP Request
    |
    v
JWT Authentication middleware validates token
    |
    v
FastEndpoints routes to endpoint
    |
    v
Request deserialization + FluentValidation
    |
    v
TenantEndpoint.HandleAsync extracts TenantId/UserId/RequestLanguageId/DefaultLanguageId
    |  (returns 401 if claims missing)
    v
HandleValidatedRequestAsync executes
    a. Execute command/query
    b. Check result.Succeeded (all command types)
       - !Succeeded → SendProblemAsync(result.Error) + return
    c. For save: check result.Value!.NewItems vs UpdatedItems
       - NewItems.Count > 0 → audit "created" + 201
       - else → audit "updated" + 200
    d. For delete/lifecycle: audit + Send response (204 for delete, 200/204 for lifecycle)

rules.rule_text: Use primary constructors for dependency injection
Extend a `TenantEndpoint` base class (never raw `Endpoint<T>` for management API)
Keep endpoints thin: execute command/query, check result, and send the HTTP response.
Audit all state changes through command-owned IAuditService; pass the active shared ActionsDbContext when audit rows must share the transaction.
Return 404 when entity not found
Save commands return `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>`; check `result.Succeeded`, then `result.Value!.NewItems.Count > 0` for 201 vs 200
Delete/lifecycle commands return `CommandResult` or `CommandResult<T>`; check `result.Succeeded`, call `SendProblemAsync` on failure
Use `SaveRequest` that inherits from the entity DTO; `Guid.Empty` means create (from `KeyedDTO`)
Return 201 Created for save-create, 200 OK for save-update
Run data and count queries in parallel via `Task.WhenAll` for grid list endpoints
Use DI-registered mapper classes (`dtoMapper.Map(entity, ...)`) — not extension method mappers
Use POST for grid list endpoints with filter payload in request body
Check cross-tenant authorization before executing the command (fail fast)
Use `Send.CreatedAtAsync<GetEndpoint>()` for 201 responses to set the `Location` header
Put business logic in endpoints (delegate to Commands/Queries)
Return raw EF entities (always map via DI-registered mapper classes — see [DTO Mapper](dto-mapper.md))
Use separate Create and Update endpoints (use Save upsert pattern)
Use legacy POST /api/v1/{entities}/delete for new Actions admin delete endpoints without an explicit ADR/design exception; use the DELETE route contracts in PAT-CORE-API-ENDPOINTS.
Use `OneOf<>` for any command result matching — use `EntityMapperResult<T>` for save, `CommandResult`/`CommandResult<T>` for delete/lifecycle
Detect create-vs-update by checking `req.Id` in the endpoint (the `EntityMapperResult` tells you)
Use GET for grid list endpoints (use POST to carry filter payload)
Use extension method mappers (`entity.ToDTO()`) — use DI-registered mapper classes

Delete requests must include concurrency data: bulk admin deletes use Items[] with Id and RowVersion, with documented Version fallback only for versioned configuration rows; single aggregate deletes use route id plus RowVersion body.
Use /lookups for new admin capture lookup aggregate endpoints; reserve singular /lookup for documented legacy or non-admin single-list endpoints.

sections.title: Endpoint Categories
content: | Management API | `/api/v1/*` | JWT + claims | TenantEndpoint base |
| M2M Service | `/api/v1/*` | M2M token | TenantEndpoint base |

sections.title: TenantEndpoint Base Class
content: All management endpoints extend one of four base class variants. Each validates JWT claims, extracts session context (`TenantId`, `UserId`, `RequestLanguageId`, `DefaultLanguageId`), and delegates to `HandleValidatedRequestAsync`.
public abstract class TenantEndpoint<TRequest, TResponse> : Endpoint<TRequest, TResponse>
protected Guid TenantId { get; private set; }
protected Guid UserId { get; private set; }
protected Guid RequestLanguageId { get; private set; }
protected Guid DefaultLanguageId { get; private set; }
public sealed override async Task HandleAsync(TRequest req, CancellationToken ct)
HttpContext, out var tenantId, out var userId,
out var requestLanguageId, out var defaultLanguageId))
protected abstract Task HandleValidatedRequestAsync(TRequest req, CancellationToken ct);
// Request only, no response (Delete, Lifecycle endpoints)
// Response only, no request (Get single entity)
// No request or response (Lifecycle with no body)

Claims are extracted in `TenantEndpointHelper.TryExtractClaims` via `NxgnClaimTypes.TenantId`, `ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier` (falling back to `"sub"`), `NxgnClaimTypes.RequestLanguageId`, and `NxgnClaimTypes.DefaultLanguageId`. Missing or unparseable `TenantId`/`UserId` returns 401 Unauthorized. Language IDs fall back to system defaults if missing claims.
| Save | `TenantEndpoint<TRequest, TResponse>` | Request body + response with ID |
| Get | `TenantEndpointWithoutRequest<TResponse>` | Route param only, no request body |
| Lookup | `TenantEndpointWithoutRequest<TResponse>` | No request body, returns list |
| Delete | `TenantEndpoint<TRequest>` | Request body with delete contract, no response |
| Lifecycle (with response) | `TenantEndpoint<TRequest, TResponse>` | Request body + DTO response |
| Lifecycle (no response) | `TenantEndpointWithoutRequest` | Route param only, 204 No Content |

sections.title: Endpoint Type Matrix
content: | Type | Verb | Route | Response Code | Example |
| Delete (single admin aggregate) | DELETE | `/api/v1/admin/{entities}/{id}` | 204 No Content | `DELETE /api/v1/admin/compliance-profiles/{id}` |

sections.title: SaveEndpoint — Upsert Pattern
content: Save handles both create and update via a single POST. The `SaveRequest` inherits from the entity DTO (see [Requests](requests.md)); the `Id` property (from `KeyedDTO`) determines the operation: `Guid.Empty` = create, non-empty `Guid` = update. The command returns `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>`; the endpoint checks `result.Succeeded` first, then inspects `result.Value!.NewItems.Count > 0` to determine whether it was a create (201) or update (200).
The command owns persistence, validation, domain side effects, and audit logging. The endpoint derives only the HTTP response status and response payload from the command result.

`SaveRequest` extends the entity DTO, inheriting all fields including `Id` from `KeyedDTO`. Most save requests add no extra fields - the inheritance is for type separation and the ability to add write-only fields when needed:
public string Slug { get; set; } = string.Empty;
public EnumDTO ActionType { get; set; } = null!;
public EnumDTO Status { get; set; } = null!;

Create vs update is determined by `Id` (inherited from `KeyedDTO`): `Guid.Empty` = create, non-empty = update. The `EntityMapper` checks this and returns `EntityMapperResult<T>` with the entity in either `NewItems` or `UpdatedItems`.

The endpoint checks `result.Succeeded` first. On failure, `SendProblemAsync` maps the error to the correct HTTP status. On success, the endpoint inspects the `EntityMapperResult` to determine create vs update:
| `!result.Succeeded` | From `result.Error.StatusCode` | Command returned a domain error (404, 409, 422) |
| `mapResult.NewItems.Count > 0` | 201 Created | EntityMapper created a new entity |
| `mapResult.UpdatedItems.Count > 0` | 200 OK | EntityMapper updated an existing entity |
For single-entity save endpoints, exactly one of these lists will have one item. The endpoint reads the entity ID from whichever list is populated.
All expected error conditions are expressed as `CommandResult.Fail(...)` by the command - the endpoint never needs to catch exceptions for domain logic:
- **Update target not found**: Command detects empty mapper result -> `Fail(404)`
- **Slug/uniqueness conflicts**: Command detects duplicate eagerly -> `Fail(409)`, or TOCTOU catch -> `Fail(409)`
See [Commands](commands.md) for the full save command flow.
### Correct vs Wrong: Save Result Handling
// CORRECT - check Succeeded first, then inspect EntityMapperResult
var result = await saveCommand.ExecuteAsync(req, UserId, RequestLanguageId, DefaultLanguageId, ct);
// WRONG - accessing EntityMapperResult without checking Succeeded
var result = await saveCommand.ExecuteAsync(req, UserId, RequestLanguageId, DefaultLanguageId, ct);
var isNew = result.Value!.NewItems.Count > 0;  // NullReferenceException if command failed
// WRONG - checking Guid.Empty on request (leaks detection into endpoint)

sections.title: Canonical Slice Naming
content: Endpoint route shapes are contract-driven, but endpoint and slice names must follow the platform's canonical feature-slice naming pattern. See `PAT-CORE-FEATURE-SLICE-NAMING`.
- prefer `Get...Lookups` for admin capture aggregate lookup routes and `Get...Lookup` / `Get...LookupList` for single-list lookup routes
- admin capture routes use `/lookups`; singular `/lookup` is reserved for documented legacy, non-admin, or downstream-owned contracts
- when a downstream contract uses `/grid` or `/lookup`, reflect that exact route in lex and implementation instead of normalizing it to an older local route shape

sections.title: GetEndpoint — Single Entity
content: Get endpoints use `TenantEndpointWithoutRequest<TResponse>` and extract the ID from the route. They return 200 with the DTO or 404.
if (action is null) { await Send.NotFoundAsync(ct); return; }

Get endpoints use `TenantEndpointWithoutRequest<T>` (not `TenantEndpoint<TReq, TResp>`) because the only input is a route parameter, not a request body. Mapping uses DI-registered mapper classes (`dtoMapper.Map(entity, ...)`) — see [DTO Mapper](dto-mapper.md).

sections.title: GetGridListEndpoint — Paginated List
content: Grid list endpoints use POST to carry the filter payload in the request body (matching Capstone). They accept `PagedRequest` via POST body and return `GridListResponse<TGridItemDTO>`. They run a data query and a count query in parallel via `Task.WhenAll`, then combine the results.

GridList uses POST (not GET) because: (1) filter payloads can be complex (nested filters, multi-select, sorting), (2) POST body avoids query string length limits, and (3) it matches the Capstone convention. The route uses `/grid-list` suffix to distinguish from the Save endpoint on the same entity path.
// Provides pagination and common search for simple entities
When an entity needs filters beyond Search and Status, create a feature-specific request that inherits from `PagedRequest`:
The endpoint uses the feature-specific request type and maps all fields to QueryParameters:
Use `PagedRequest` directly for entities with only Search and Status filters. Extend it when the entity needs additional filter fields.
### Correct vs Wrong: DTO Mapping in Grid Lists
// CORRECT — DI-registered mapper class with batch method

sections.title: DeleteEndpoint - Admin Delete Contracts
content: Admin delete endpoints return 204 No Content on success and delegate protection, reference, and concurrency checks to a delete command returning `CommandResult`. The request contract must include the selected entity id or ids plus concurrency data. Missing or stale concurrency data is a validation/concurrency failure, not a best-effort delete.
Actions admin modules intentionally allow two delete route shapes:

| Bulk delete from a server-grid list | `DELETE /api/v1/admin/{entities}` | `Delete{Entities}Request` with `Items[]` containing `Id` and `RowVersion`; versioned configuration rows may also include `Version` as a documented compatibility fallback | Action Types, RCA Methods, Escalation Rules, Deadline Types |
| Single aggregate delete | `DELETE /api/v1/admin/{entities}/{id}` | Route `id` plus `Delete{Entity}Request` body containing `RowVersion` | Compliance Profiles, Workflow Drafts |

Use collection-level `DELETE` when the UX supports multi-select deletion or when deleting lookup/configuration rows from a list. Use entity-level `DELETE` when the command is inherently for one aggregate, usually from a capture or lifecycle surface, and the id is already part of the route contract. Do not copy the entity-level route into a grid bulk-delete endpoint just to avoid a bulk request wrapper.

1. Validate request shape: at least one id, max batch size where applicable, no duplicate ids, and row-version concurrency data for every selected row. Escalation Rules and Deadline Types additionally accept `Version` while the UI still sends both `Version` and `RowVersion`.
2. Load only tenant-owned rows and return 404 when any requested row is missing.
3. Compare `RowVersion` or documented `Version` fallback before any destructive change; stale concurrency data returns 409.
4. Enforce seed/system protection and ADR-CORE-0020 RESTRICT blockers; return 422 for domain-valid but non-deletable requests.
5. Delete owned children inside the aggregate, hard-delete simple configuration rows, or soft-delete versioned/configuration rows where ADR-CORE-0020 requires retention or restore semantics.
6. Audit inside the command after validation succeeds; pass the shared `ActionsDbContext` to `IAuditService` when audit rows must participate in the same transaction.

Both route shapes use FastEndpoints `Delete(...)` and `TenantEndpoint<TRequest>`. HTTP DELETE request bodies are acceptable for Actions admin APIs because the BFF and Angular clients are under our control. External/public APIs may choose a different route shape, but that exception must be documented in an ADR or technical design before implementation.

sections.title: LookupEndpoint — Dropdown Data
content: Lookup endpoints provide lightweight data for dropdowns and selection controls. Admin capture pages use plural `/lookups` because a capture page usually needs multiple lookup lists in one response. Legacy/non-admin single-list endpoints may still use singular `/lookup`, but new admin modules should use `/lookups`.

Lookup endpoints differ from GetGridList in three ways: (1) no pagination, (2) minimal DTO or capture lookup aggregate response, (3) authorization matches the capture/list surface using the lookup data. Optional query parameters may filter the list.
Lookup and enum endpoints set `Cache-Control` headers only when the data is safe to cache. Grid endpoints are never cached.
| `GET /api/v1/admin/{entities}/lookups` | usually no caching | Capture lookup data can include tenant configuration and permission-dependent options |
| `GET /api/v1/{resource}/lookup` | `private, max-age=60` when explicitly safe | Legacy/single-list tenant-scoped lookup cache |

sections.title: Lifecycle/Action Endpoints
content: Lifecycle endpoints perform specific state transitions on an entity. They use POST with the entity ID in the route and the action as the last segment. The command returns `CommandResult` (no data) or `CommandResult<T>` (with data). The endpoint checks `result.Succeeded` and calls `SendProblemAsync` on failure.
When the caller needs the updated entity state after a lifecycle transition, the endpoint re-loads the entity and maps it via the DI-registered DTOMapper:
var result = await activateCommand.ExecuteAsync(id, UserId, ct);
var result = await archiveCommand.ExecuteAsync(id, TenantId, UserId, ct);

Lifecycle commands own persistence and audit logging. Lifecycle endpoints return 200 with a DTO when the caller needs the updated state, or 204 when they do not. Base class choice follows the same rule: with response body use `TenantEndpoint<TReq, TResp>`, without use `TenantEndpointWithoutRequest`. Both check `result.Succeeded` before proceeding.

sections.title: Audit Logging
content: All state-changing commands log via `IAuditService` after validation succeeds and before the transaction commits when the audit record must be atomic with the write.
- `action`: dotted or Pascal-cased domain action used by audit consumers.
- `dbContext`: pass the active shared context for transactional audit writes.
Endpoints stay thin: they execute the command/query, check `result.Succeeded`, call `SendProblemAsync` on failure, and send the HTTP response. They do not duplicate command-owned audit loops.

sections.title: Request Lifecycle
content: c. For save: check result.Value!.NewItems vs UpdatedItems
d. For delete/lifecycle: send response (204 for delete, 200/204 for lifecycle)
Commands own domain validation, persistence, and audit logging. Endpoints do not duplicate command-owned audit work.

sections.title: Route Conventions
content: | `POST /api/v1/{entities}/grid-list` | Grid list with pagination (filter payload in body) |
| `GET /api/v1/{entities}/lookup` | Legacy or non-admin lightweight single-list lookup |
| `DELETE /api/v1/admin/{entities}/{id}` | Single admin aggregate delete with route id + `RowVersion` body |

sections.title: Rules
content: 1. Use primary constructors for dependency injection.
2. Extend a `TenantEndpoint` base class (never raw `Endpoint<T>` for management API).
3. Keep endpoints thin: execute command/query, check result, respond.
4. Audit all state changes through command-owned `IAuditService`; pass the active shared `ActionsDbContext` when audit rows must share the transaction.
6. Save commands return `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>` or the module-specific save result contract; check `result.Succeeded` before reading the value.
7. Delete/lifecycle commands return `CommandResult` or `CommandResult<T>`; check `result.Succeeded`, call `SendProblemAsync` on failure.
8. Use `SaveRequest` that inherits from the entity DTO; `Guid.Empty` means create (from `KeyedDTO`).
9. Return 201 Created for save-create where the endpoint has a single-entity Location contract, otherwise follow the module save result convention documented in design.
10. Run data and count queries in parallel via `Task.WhenAll` for grid list endpoints.
11. Use DI-registered mapper classes (`dtoMapper.Map(entity, ...)`) - not extension method mappers.
12. Use POST for grid list endpoints with filter payload in request body.
13. Use `/lookups` for new admin capture lookup aggregate endpoints.

14. Delete requests must include concurrency data: bulk admin deletes use `Items[]` with `Id` and `RowVersion`, with documented `Version` fallback only for versioned configuration rows; single aggregate deletes use route id plus `RowVersion` body.
1. Check cross-tenant authorization before executing the command (fail fast).
2. Use `Send.CreatedAtAsync<GetEndpoint>()` for 201 responses to set the `Location` header when the module uses 201 create semantics.
1. Put business logic in endpoints (delegate to Commands/Queries).
2. Return raw EF entities (always map via DI-registered mapper classes - see [DTO Mapper](api-dto-mapper.md)).
3. Use separate Create and Update endpoints (use Save upsert pattern).
4. Use legacy `POST /api/v1/{entities}/delete` for new Actions admin delete endpoints without an explicit ADR/design exception.
5. Use `OneOf<>` for any command result matching - use `EntityMapperResult<T>` for save, `CommandResult`/`CommandResult<T>` for delete/lifecycle.
6. Detect create-vs-update by checking `req.Id` in the endpoint when an `EntityMapperResult` is available.
7. Use GET for grid list endpoints (use POST to carry filter payload).
8. Use extension method mappers (`entity.ToDTO()`) - use DI-registered mapper classes.
